1: vayehi miyamim rabim achar asher heniyach YHWH leYisrael mikol oyveihem misaviv: ‘a long time afterward, when the LORD had given rest to Israel from all their surrounding enemies’ — Joshua’s farewell speech is set in old age, after the rest has been established. The menuha (rest) is both military and covenantal: the land is secured, the Levitical cities distributed, the covenant renewed at Ebal. Joshua speaks from the vantage point of accomplished mission. Hebrews 4:8 cites this rest as insufficient: “for if Joshua had given them rest, God would not have spoken of another day later on.” The rest of the completed conquest points beyond itself.
2: Joshua summons all Israel — their elders, heads, judges, and officers — the full leadership structure of the covenant community. The formality of the summons matches the gravity of what follows: a farewell speech of a leader who has completed his work, knows he is dying, and needs to leave the community with the essential word. The succession of Moses and Joshua both end with farewell addresses (Deut 31-34; Josh 23-24): the pattern of the dying leader’s final testimony is foundational to the covenant tradition.
3: atem re'item et kol asher asah YHWH Eloheichem lekhol hagoyim ha'eleh mifinechem ki YHWH Eloheichem hu hanilochem lachem: ‘you yourselves have seen all that the LORD your God has done to all these nations — for it is the LORD your God who has fought for you’ — the appeal to direct experience (atem re'item, you yourselves have seen) grounds the farewell in testimony rather than argument. Joshua does not deduce or reason to divine faithfulness; he points to what the community witnessed. This is the consistent biblical mode of covenant instruction: redemptive history is not a doctrine to be accepted but a testimony to be received from witnesses.
4: re'u hipeladeti lachem et hagoyim hanorotim ha'eleh lenachalah leshivtechem: ‘I have allotted to you as an inheritance for your tribes these remaining nations’ — Joshua distinguishes the nations already defeated from those that remain. The promised land has been allotted even though not all of it is yet secured; Israel holds the land by right of divine allocation, not by the completion of military conquest. This is the “already and not yet” structure: the inheritance is theirs by divine gift, fully realized through ongoing obedience.
5: vaYHWH Eloheichem hu yehdofem mifinechem veyorish otam mifinechem vishatem et artzam ka'asher diber lahem YHWH Eloheichem: ‘the LORD your God will himself drive them out from before you and dispossess them — you shall possess their land, as the LORD your God promised you’ — the future completion of what is already begun rests on divine initiative (hu yehdofem, he himself will drive them). Israel’s task is faithfulness; the driving-out is YHWH’s action. The distinction between human covenant fidelity and divine accomplishment is maintained even in the military language.
6: vachazaqtem me'od lishmor vela'asot et kol hakatuv besefer torat Moshe lebilti sur mimenu yamin usmol: ‘be very strong to keep and to do all that is written in the Book of the Law of Moses, turning aside from it neither to the right nor to the left’ — Joshua echoes his own commission (1:7: “do not turn from it to the right or to the left”). The charge he received at YHWH’s call he now passes to all Israel at his death. The sefer torat Moshe (Book of the Law of Moses) is the written Torah as the permanent deposit of covenantal obligation. Obedience to written Torah — not to oral tradition or to national interest — is the standard.
7: lebilti lavo bago'im ha'eleh hanorotim ha'eleh ushvem lo tizkiru: ‘so that you may not mix with these nations remaining among you, nor make mention of the names of their gods’ — the separation principle: not merely avoiding worship of other gods, but not even invoking their names. The prohibition on naming (lo tizkiru) reflects the covenantal significance of naming: to invoke a name is to acknowledge a power, to enter a relationship of dependence. The same principle appears in Paul’s instruction: “whatever is true, whatever is honorable… think about these things” (Phil 4:8) — cognitive separateness from false allegiances precedes behavioral separateness.
8: ki im beYHWH Eloheichem tidbaqun ka'asher asitem ad hayom hazeh: ‘but you shall cling to the LORD your God, as you have done to this day’ — dabaq (cling) is the verb of intimate attachment: Gen 2:24 (“a man shall cling to his wife”), Ruth 1:14 (“Ruth clung to her”). The covenant relationship with YHWH requires the same total, exclusive, intimate attachment as marriage. The phrase “as you have done to this day” is both commendation and standard: continue what has characterized you so far. The farewell speech assumes continuity with the achieved faithfulness of the conquest generation.
9: vayorish YHWH mifinechem goyim gedolim ve'atsumim: ‘the LORD drove out before you great and strong nations’ — the completed-tense summary of what YHWH did is the basis for what YHWH will continue to do. The God who drove out great nations for Israel is not less capable now; the appeal to divine track record is an appeal to divine character, which does not change. The rhetorical function is identical to Paul’s argument in Rom 8:32: “He who did not spare his own Son but gave him up for us all, how will he not also with him graciously give us all things?”
10: ish echad miachem yirdof elef ki YHWH Eloheichem hu hanilochem lachem ka'asher diber lachem: ‘one man of you puts a thousand to flight, because the LORD your God fights for you, as he promised you’ — the arithmetic of divine empowerment: one warrior plus YHWH equals a thousand opponents. The mathematics is not military but covenantal — it measures not force-ratios but the asymmetry between human weakness and divine strength. Lev 26:8 promises the same disproportionate effectiveness: “five of you shall chase a hundred, and a hundred of you shall chase ten thousand.”
11: venishmartem me'od le'nafshoteichem le'ahavah et YHWH Eloheichem: ‘be very careful to love the LORD your God’ — the charge to love (ahavah) frames covenant obedience as affection, not merely legal compliance. The Shema’s command to love YHWH with all heart, soul, and strength (Deut 6:5) is the ground of all specific obligations; Joshua’s farewell charge returns to this foundation. The specific dangers (vv12-13) follow from this positive command: they are threats to the love-relationship, not merely to legal standing.
12: vechi shuv tashuvu udevaqtem beyeter hagoyim ha'eleh hanorotim ha'eleh itchem uhitchatantem bahem uveim bahem veyim bachem: ‘for if you turn back and cling to the remnant of these nations remaining among you and make marriages with them’ — the prohibited mixing is precisely at the point where covenant love is expressed most intimately: marriage. The Deuteronomic prohibition of intermarriage (Deut 7:3-4) is grounded not in ethnic purity but in covenant fidelity: intermarriage with those who worship other gods will turn your heart away (Deut 7:4). Dabaq (cling) appears here negatively — clinging to the nations rather than to YHWH is the betrayal of the marital metaphor of v8.
13: yada tede'u ki lo yoshif YHWH Eloheichem lehoresh et hagoyim ha'eleh mifinechem vehay lahem lepach ulemokesh uleshot betzidchem ulecicotzim be'einechem: ‘know for certain that the LORD your God will no longer drive out these nations before you — they shall be a snare and a trap for you, a whip on your sides and thorns in your eyes until you perish from this good land’ — the covenantal warning is vivid and cumulative: pach (snare), mokesh (trap), shot (whip), tzikotzim (thorns). The fourfold image of entanglement, blinding, and pain captures the progressive degradation of a community that allows idolatrous influence into its life. The language is repeated in Judg 2:3, where the fulfillment of the warning is announced.
14: vehinei anochi holech hayom bederech kol ha'aretz veyeda'atem bechol levavchem uvechol nafshechem ki lo nafal davar echad mikol hadvarim hatovim asher diber YHWH Eloheichem aleichem hakol ba lachem: ‘behold, I am going today the way of all the earth, and you know in your hearts and souls, all of you, that not one word has failed of all the good things that the LORD your God promised concerning you — all have come to pass’ — Joshua’s death-announcement (“I am going today the way of all the earth”) precedes the final testimony. The dying man’s witness to divine faithfulness is the most credible: a person about to stand before YHWH has no reason to exaggerate divine goodness. Lo nafal davar (not one word fell) — repeating 21:45 at the farewell speech’s climax — is the book’s central claim and theological heart.
15: vehayah ka'asher ba aleichem kol hadavar hatov asher diber YHWH Eloheichem aleichem ken yavi YHWH aleichem et kol hadavar hara ad hashmiido otchem me'al ha'adamah hatovah hazot asher natan lachem: ‘just as all the good things that the LORD your God promised concerning you have come to pass, so the LORD will bring upon you all the evil things until he has destroyed you from off this good land’ — the covenant is symmetric: the same divine faithfulness that fulfilled the blessings will fulfill the curses. This is not punitive cruelty but the consistent application of covenant logic: YHWH’s word is reliable in both directions. Hebrews 10:28-29 applies the same logic to the new covenant: if Torah-violation brought certain judgment, how much more will rejection of the Son.
16: baavrechem et brit YHWH Eloheichem asher tzivah etchem vahalacitem va'avaditem elohim acherim vehishtachavitem lahem vacharah af YHWH bachem ve'avadtem meherah me'al ha'aretz hatovah asher natan lachem: ‘if you transgress the covenant of the LORD your God, which he commanded you, and go and serve other gods and bow down to them — then the anger of the LORD will be kindled against you, and you shall perish quickly from off the good land that he has given you’ — the final verse of the farewell speech is the conditional that governs everything: covenant transgression → idolatry → divine anger → loss of the land. The entire history of Israel from Joshua to the exile is the story of this conditional being fulfilled. The “good land” (ha'aretz hatovah) frames both the gift (v15, asher natan lachem) and the potential loss: what is given as covenant gift is forfeited by covenant breach. The farewell ends where it began — with the covenant as the absolute center of Israel’s existence.
